data.title: Astra Microwave Products Limited Just Missed Earnings And Its Revenue Numbers Were Weaker Than Expected
data.description:
The third-quarter results for Astra Microwave Products Limited (NSE:ASTRAMICRO) were released last week, making it a good time to revisit its performance. Results look mixed - while revenue fell marginally short of analyst estimates at ₹2.6b, statutory earnings were in line with expectations, at ₹16.17 per share. This is an important time for investors, as they can track a company's performance in its report, look at what experts are forecasting for next year, and see if there has been any change to expectations for the business. We thought readers would find it interesting to see the analysts latest (statutory) post-earnings forecasts for next year.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Taking into account the latest results, the current consensus from Astra Microwave Products' five analysts is for revenues of ₹14.0b in 2027. This would reflect a substantial 29% increase on its revenue over the past 12 months. Statutory earnings per share are predicted to bounce 38% to ₹22.90. Yet prior to the latest earnings, the analysts had been anticipated revenues of ₹14.6b and earnings per share (EPS) of ₹23.53 in 2027. The analysts are less bullish than they were before these results, given the reduced revenue forecasts and the small dip in earnings per share expectations.

View our latest analysis for Astra Microwave Products

Despite the cuts to forecast earnings, there was no real change to the ₹1,170 price target, showing that the analysts don't think the changes have a meaningful impact on its intrinsic value. That's not the only conclusion we can draw from this data however, as some investors also like to consider the spread in estimates when evaluating analyst price targets. The most optimistic Astra Microwave Products analyst has a price target of ₹1,452 per share, while the most pessimistic values it at ₹1,028. Analysts definitely have varying views on the business, but the spread of estimates is not wide enough in our view to suggest that extreme outcomes could await Astra Microwave Products shareholders.

These estimates are interesting, but it can be useful to paint some more broad strokes when seeing how forecasts compare, both to the Astra Microwave Products' past performance and to peers in the same industry. It's clear from the latest estimates that Astra Microwave Products' rate of growth is expected to accelerate meaningfully, with the forecast 23% annualised revenue growth to the end of 2027 noticeably faster than its historical growth of 11% p.a. over the past five years. Compare this with other companies in the same industry, which are forecast to see revenue growth of 29% annually. So it's clear that despite the acceleration in growth, Astra Microwave Products is expected to grow meaningfully slower than the industry average.

Advertisement

The Bottom Line

The biggest concern is that the analysts reduced their earnings per share estimates, suggesting business headwinds could lay ahead for Astra Microwave Products. Unfortunately, they also downgraded their revenue estimates, and our data indicates underperformance compared to the wider industry. Even so, earnings per share are more important to the intrinsic value of the business. The consensus price target held steady at ₹1,170, with the latest estimates not enough to have an impact on their price targets.

With that in mind, we wouldn't be too quick to come to a conclusion on Astra Microwave Products. Long-term earnings power is much more important than next year's profits. data.title: Investors Should Be Encouraged By Bharat Electronics' (NSE:BEL) Returns On Capital
data.description:
To find a multi-bagger stock, what are the underlying trends we should look for in a business? One common approach is to try and find a company with returns on capital employed (ROCE) that are increasing, in conjunction with a growing amount of capital employed. This shows us that it's a compounding machine, able to continually reinvest its earnings back into the business and generate higher returns. So when we looked at the ROCE trend of Bharat Electronics (NSE:BEL) we really liked what we saw.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Advertisement

Understanding Return On Capital Employed (ROCE)

For those that aren't sure what ROCE is, it measures the amount of pre-tax profits a company can generate from the capital employed in its business. The formula for this calculation on Bharat Electronics is:

Return on Capital Employed = Earnings Before Interest and Tax (EBIT) ÷ (Total Assets - Current Liabilities)

0.32 = ₹74b ÷ (₹429b - ₹202b) (Based on the trailing twelve months to December 2025).

Thus, Bharat Electronics has an ROCE of 32%. That's a fantastic return and not only that, it outpaces the average of 17% earned by companies in a similar industry.

See our latest analysis for Bharat Electronics

Above you can see how the current ROCE for Bharat Electronics compares to its prior returns on capital, but there's only so much you can tell from the past. If you're interested, you can view the analysts predictions in our free analyst report for Bharat Electronics .

What Can We Tell From Bharat Electronics' ROCE Trend?

Bharat Electronics is displaying some positive trends. Over the last five years, returns on capital employed have risen substantially to 32%. The company is effectively making more money per dollar of capital used, and it's worth noting that the amount of capital has increased too, by 95%. So we're very much inspired by what we're seeing at Bharat Electronics thanks to its ability to profitably reinvest capital.

Another thing to note, Bharat Electronics has a high ratio of current liabilities to total assets of 47%. This effectively means that suppliers (or short-term creditors) are funding a large portion of the business, so just be aware that this can introduce some elements of risk. Ideally we'd like to see this reduce as that would mean fewer obligations bearing risks.

The Bottom Line On Bharat Electronics' ROCE

To sum it up, Bharat Electronics has proven it can reinvest in the business and generate higher returns on that capital employed, which is terrific. Since the stock has returned a staggering 938% to shareholders over the last five years, it looks like investors are recognizing these changes. Therefore, we think it would be worth your time to check if these trends are going to continue.

Bharat Electronics does have some risks though, and we've spotted 1 warning sign for Bharat Electronics that you might be interested in.

data.title: PAMT (PAMT) FY 2025 Loss Deepens As Margins Struggle Despite US$141 Million Q4 Revenue
data.description:
PAMT just posted its FY 2025 numbers with Q4 revenue of US$141.3 million and a basic EPS loss of US$1.40, while net income excluding extra items came in at a loss of US$29.3 million. The company has seen quarterly revenue move from US$166.5 million and a basic EPS loss of US$1.45 in FY 2024 Q4 to US$141.3 million and a US$1.40 loss per share in FY 2025 Q4. Trailing twelve month revenue was US$598.1 million with a cumulative EPS loss of US$2.48. For investors, the key point this quarter is how much of that revenue is reaching the bottom line and what these widening losses may suggest about margins going forward.

See our full analysis for PAMT.

US$52.6 million loss over the last year

  • On a trailing twelve month basis to FY 2025 Q4, PAMT reported total revenue of US$598.1 million and a net income loss excluding extra items of US$52.6 million, so the business is generating several hundred million dollars of sales while still ending the year in the red.
  • Bears highlight that earnings have declined at an annualized rate of 51.1% over the past five years, and the latest trailing loss of US$52.6 million reinforces that concern, although:
    • Recent quarters show losses in every period of FY 2025, ranging from US$5.6 million in Q3 to US$29.3 million in Q4, which lines up with the view that profitability has been under consistent pressure.
    • The shift from a small profit of US$2.4 million in FY 2024 Q3 to losses across the following six reported periods supports the bearish focus on earnings volatility and the absence of sustained profitability in the current data set.

Revenue holding near US$600 million while margins stay weak

  • Trailing twelve month revenue has moved from US$728.3 million at FY 2024 Q3 to US$598.1 million at FY 2025 Q4, while the trailing loss widened from US$2.4 million to US$52.6 million over the same snapshots, so the gap between sales and profits has remained wide even as the revenue base stays in the hundreds of millions.
  • What stands out for the cautious view is that every quarter in FY 2025 combined more than US$141 million of revenue per quarter with a loss excluding extra items, which matches the bearish narrative that higher sales alone have not translated into clear earnings strength:
    • Quarterly revenue across FY 2025 ranged from US$141.3 million to US$155.3 million, but each of those quarters still recorded a net loss, suggesting that current cost levels are absorbing that revenue.
    • The FY 2025 Q4 EPS loss of US$1.40, alongside earlier FY 2025 EPS losses between US$0.27 and US$0.46, supports the view that margins remain under pressure across the full year rather than in a single isolated period.

P/S of 0.4x versus industry on 12 month losses

  • PAMT is trading on a P/S ratio of 0.4x based on the latest data, compared with 1.2x for the broader US Transportation industry and 0.6x for peers, so the market is valuing each dollar of PAMT's sales at a discount while the company remains loss making on US$52.6 million of trailing twelve month net income loss.
  • Supporters who focus on that lower P/S multiple sometimes point to potential value, but the analysis data raises two clear checks on a bullish angle:
    • The company is unprofitable on a trailing basis and its losses have grown at an annualized rate of 51.1% over five years, which can make a low P/S ratio look more like a reflection of risk than a signal of hidden strength.
    • Debt coverage by operating cash flow is described as weak in the risk summary, so any bullish argument built purely on the 0.4x P/S needs to factor in that the current earnings and cash profile do not yet back up a simple value story.

data.title: Trupanion (TRUP) Turns TTM Profitable Yet Thin Margins Test Bullish Narratives
data.description:
Trupanion (TRUP) just wrapped FY 2025 with Q4 revenue of US$376.9 million and basic EPS of US$0.13, while trailing twelve month EPS sat at US$0.45 on revenue of about US$1.4 billion. The company has seen revenue move from US$337.3 million in Q4 2024 to US$376.9 million in Q4 2025, with quarterly basic EPS shifting from US$0.04 a year ago to US$0.13 most recently. This puts the focus squarely on how sustainably margins are settling at these levels.

See our full analysis for Trupanion.

With the latest figures on the table, the next step is to weigh these margins and growth trends against the widely followed Trupanion narratives to see which views are supported and which might need a rethink.

See what the community is saying about Trupanion

Advertisement

TTM earnings swing from loss to US$19.4 million profit

  • On a trailing twelve month basis, Trupanion moved from a net loss of US$9.6 million in Q4 2024 to net income of US$19.4 million in Q4 2025, with TTM EPS shifting from a loss of US$0.23 to a profit of US$0.45 over the same window.
  • What stands out for the bullish narrative is that this move into profitability lines up with its argument that higher retention and direct pay technology can support better margins. However:
    • TTM revenue rose from US$1.29b in Q4 2024 to US$1.44b in Q4 2025 while earnings stayed relatively modest at US$19.4 million, which leaves plenty of room between current margins and the bulls' expectation of higher long term profitability.
    • Bulls point to reinvestment and new products as future earnings drivers, but the current TTM margin implied by US$19.4 million of profit on US$1.44b of revenue shows that most of the optimistic upside they talk about is not yet visible in the reported numbers.

Stronger TTM profit with only a thin margin leaves plenty of questions about how far the bullish case can really stretch from here, which is exactly what dedicated bull analysts are debating in their detailed narrative on Trupanion. 🐂 Trupanion Bull Case

High 62.4x P/E against 13.2% earnings growth forecasts

  • The shares trade on a trailing P/E of 62.4x while the company's five year earnings growth rate is cited at 25.5% annually and forward earnings growth is forecast at about 13.2% per year, so the multiple sits well above the US insurance industry average of 12.3x and a peer average of 17.5x.
  • Skeptics highlight this valuation gap as a key risk, arguing that paying 62.4x earnings with forecast earnings growth of around 13.2% and revenue growth near 8.6% leaves little room for disappointment. However:
    • Trupanion has only recently turned profitable on a TTM basis, so some of that high P/E reflects the low current earnings base rather than a long record of high margins that would already justify a premium.
    • The bearish view leans heavily on rising competition and cost pressures, but the current profitability and high earnings quality rating in the data show that, so far, those pressures have not pushed the company back into losses.

A rich 62.4x P/E with modest forecast growth is exactly the kind of setup bearish analysts focus on when they map out downside scenarios for Trupanion's story. 🐻 Trupanion Bear Case

Quarterly profit steadies after early year loss

  • Across FY 2025, Trupanion went from a Q1 net loss of US$1.5 million and basic EPS of US$0.03 loss to positive net income in each of the next three quarters, ending Q4 with US$5.6 million of net income and basic EPS of US$0.13 on US$376.9 million of revenue.
  • The consensus narrative suggests improved underwriting and tech driven efficiencies are helping margins, and this pattern of three profitable quarters after an early loss fits that story. However:
    • Q2 2025 showed the strongest basic EPS at US$0.22 and net income of US$9.4 million, compared with US$5.9 million in Q3 and US$5.6 million in Q4, so profitability has not moved in a straight line.
    • With Q4 revenue at US$376.9 million versus US$337.3 million a year earlier and quarterly EPS at US$0.13 versus US$0.04, the numbers support the idea of better earnings power, although the relatively small profit against more than US$300 million of revenue keeps attention on how durable those margins really are.

Approach 1: FirstEnergy Dividend Discount Model (DDM) Analysis

The Dividend Discount Model estimates what a stock might be worth by projecting all future dividends and discounting them back to today. It is essentially asking what a fair price could be if you are mainly buying for the dividend stream.

For FirstEnergy, the model uses a recent annual dividend per share of about US$2.00, a return on equity of 9.15% and a very high payout ratio of roughly 99.18%. That combination leaves only a small portion of earnings to reinvest, which leads the model to a very low expected dividend growth rate of around 0.07%. The growth input is calculated as the product of retained earnings and return on equity, as Simply Wall St outlines in its DDM source.

When these dividend assumptions are run through the model, the estimated intrinsic value comes out at about US$29.04 per share. Compared with the current share price around US$50.10, that implies the stock is roughly 72.5% overvalued based purely on this dividend based framework.

Result: OVERVALUED

Approach 2: FirstEnergy Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like FirstEnergy, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to gauge what investors are currently willing to pay for each dollar of earnings. It links the share price directly to the bottom line, which is usually the core focus for equity holders.

What counts as a "normal" P/E depends on what the market expects for future growth and how risky those earnings are. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually supports a lower one.

FirstEnergy currently trades on a P/E of 21.76x. That sits below the Simply Wall St Electric Utilities peer average of 17.72x and close to the broader industry average of 22.39x. Simply Wall St also calculates a proprietary Fair Ratio of 23.08x, which reflects factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market cap and company specific risks.

This Fair Ratio can be more tailored than a simple peer or industry comparison because it adjusts for the specific profile of FirstEnergy rather than assuming all utilities deserve the same multiple. With the current P/E of 21.76x slightly below the Fair Ratio of 23.08x, the shares screen as modestly undervalued on this metric.

data.title: Westwood Holdings Group (WHG) Net Margin Jump Questioned By One Off Gain Narrative
data.description:
Westwood Holdings Group (WHG) has wrapped up FY 2025 with fourth quarter revenue of US$27.1 million and basic EPS of US$0.22, rounding out a trailing 12 month picture that includes total revenue of US$97.8 million and EPS of US$0.84. Over recent periods the company has seen revenue move from US$25.6 million and EPS of US$0.25 in Q4 2024 to Q3 2025 levels of US$24.3 million and EPS of US$0.44 before the latest quarter, while trailing net profit margin has shifted from 2.3% to 7.2%. This gives investors a clearer view of how earnings translate into margins. Overall, the release points to margins that are more supportive of the current earnings profile, but also highlights the need to separate ongoing profitability from one off items.

See our full analysis for Westwood Holdings Group.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile aligns with the dominant narratives around Westwood, and where the latest margin trends might challenge those views.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

7.2% net margin helped by US$1.9m one off

  • Over the last 12 months, net profit margin sat at 7.2% versus 2.3% a year earlier, with a US$1.9m one off gain included in the earnings that feed into that margin.
  • Bulls pointing to the 218.6% earnings growth over the past year get some support from this margin shift, yet the one off gain means:
    • Trailing 12 month net income of US$7.1m includes that US$1.9m item, so the step up in profitability is not entirely from recurring operations.
    • Quarterly EPS has also moved around within the year, from US$0.06 in Q1 2025 to US$0.44 in Q3 2025 and US$0.22 in Q4 2025, which makes it harder to treat the latest margin level as a steady run rate.

AUM steady, but flows swing by US$642m

  • Within FY 2025, reported net flows ranged from an inflow of US$642m in Q1 2025 to outflows of US$231m in Q2 2025 and US$671m in Q3 2025, while AUM moved between US$16.6b and US$17.3b over those periods.
  • For a more optimistic take, the advisory and trust franchise held AUM roughly flat despite those swings in flows, yet:
    • AUM at the start of Q3 2025 was US$17.3b and ended the period at US$17.3b, even though there was a US$671m outflow, so market performance or other factors had to offset client withdrawals.
    • Earlier in the year, Q1 2025 started at US$16.6b and ended at US$17.0b with a US$642m inflow, highlighting how sensitive revenue can be to shifts in client activity from quarter to quarter.

P/E of 20.9x sits below industry at 23.1x

  • The shares trade on a trailing P/E of 20.9x compared with 23.1x for the US Capital Markets industry and 103.4x for peers, using trailing earnings that include the US$1.9m one off gain.
  • Investors who see this as a value angle have some points in their favor, but the data also gives bears material talking points:
    • On one hand, trailing 12 month EPS of US$0.84 and a share price of US$17.69 produce that 20.9x multiple, which is lower than both the sector average and the much higher peer average.
    • On the other hand, an unstable dividend record and recent insider selling, alongside the one off gain in earnings, mean the trailing P/E is based on a mix of recurring and non recurring drivers rather than a clean earnings stream.
